content: Introduction Place a brief intro here. Each chapter typically contains: text, diagrams, code snippets, and links. See the placeholder examples below.
description: fixed size sliding window, subarray of size k, maximum sum subarray k, sliding window fixed length
summary: A sliding window approach where the window size remains constant throughout the iteration. Commonly used for problems like maximum sum subarray of size k or average of subarrays.
title: Fixed Size Window

content: Variable Size Sliding Window (Expand and Shrink Strategy) Variable size sliding window dynamically adjusts its range based on problem constraints. Unlike fixed windows, it expands to include more elements and shrinks when conditions are violated. This technique is widely used in problems like longest substring, minimum window substring, and other constraint-based scenarios.
description: variable size sliding window, dynamic window subarray, longest substring problems, sliding window expand shrink
summary: A flexible sliding window where the size expands or shrinks based on a given condition. Useful for problems like longest substring or subarray satisfying a constraint.
title: Variable Size Window

content: Introduction Place a brief intro here. Each chapter typically contains: text, diagrams, code snippets, and links. See the placeholder examples below.
description: Concise description for search engines and social previews.
summary: Enhances variable windows by using a hash map to track element frequency or presence. Essential for solving problems like longest substring without repeating characters or anagram matching.
title: Variable Size Window Plus Hashmap

content: Introduction Place a brief intro here. Each chapter typically contains: text, diagrams, code snippets, and links. See the placeholder examples below.
description: Concise description for search engines and social previews.
summary: Combines sliding window with a deque that maintains elements in sorted order (monotonic). Used to efficiently find maximum or minimum values in a window in O(n) time.
title: Window Plus Monotonic Deque

content:
Is this site really free? Yes. Every tutorial is free to read. We may show ads to cover hosting.
Do I need an account? No. Your progress is stored in your browser and never leaves your device.
How do I sync progress across devices? You can&rsquo;t — there&rsquo;s no backend. If you need cross-device sync, you&rsquo;ll have to remember where you were.
How are tutorials organized? The hierarchy is Track → Course → Chapter. Tracks are big-picture curricula; courses are focused topics; chapters are individual lessons.
Can I contribute? Send us an email — see the contact page.
description: Common questions about Tech Tutorials and how it works.
summary:

Analytics If analytics is enabled, only anonymized, aggregate metrics are collected. We do not attempt to identify individual readers.

content:
Sliding Window is a core pattern in algorithm design, especially useful for array and string problems that involve contiguous elements. Instead of repeatedly recalculating results for every possible subarray, the window “slides” across the data—expanding when needed and shrinking when constraints are violated. This reuse of previous computations makes the approach highly efficient.
There are two primary variations: fixed-size and variable-size windows. Fixed-size windows maintain a constant length, while variable-size windows dynamically grow and shrink based on conditions such as sum, …
description: sliding window technique, subarray problems, two pointers technique
summary: A technique that processes a subset (window) of elements in a list or string by moving it incrementally instead of recomputing from scratch. It is used to optimize problems involving contiguous subarrays, often reducing time complexity from O(n²) to O(n).
title: Sliding Window
